channel.title: Code Defence Intelligence Brief — Mon, 23 Mar 2026
channel.description: Daily cybersecurity intelligence for Mon, 23 Mar 2026.

item.title: CanisterWorm transitions to wiper attack targeting cloud infrastructure. 🛡️
item.description:
The threat actors behind the Trivy supply chain attack have unleashed a destructive cloud-native worm. 🛡️
Threat Alert · TeamPCP deploying CanisterWorm wiper via exposed Docker and Kubernetes APIs.
In the last 24 hours, security researchers have observed a shift in TeamPCP's tactics. The group is now deploying a new variant of CanisterWorm that executes a wiper payload if the infected system is located in the Iran timezone or has Farsi set as the default language. The worm spreads by industrializing the exploitation of exposed control planes and misconfigured cloud APIs.
If the wiper component detects that the victim is in the target region and has access to a Kubernetes cluster, it will systematically destroy data on every node in that cluster. This follows the group's recent supply chain attack on the Trivy scanner via @[GitHub](urn:li:organization:19041) Actions.
The uncomfortable truth: Your cloud-native infrastructure is no longer just a target for data theft; it is now a frontline for automated, geopolitical destructive operations.
→ Audit all @[Docker](urn:li:organization:3071271) and Kubernetes APIs to ensure they are not exposed to the public internet without strict authentication. → Rotate all cloud credentials, SSH keys, and tokens that may have been exposed during recent Trivy or npm registry scans. → Implement egress filtering on all container nodes to prevent the worm from communicating with command-and-control infrastructure.
#Cybersecurity #CloudSecurity #CanisterWorm #SupplyChain #CISO #CodeDefence

item.title: Unpatched Quest KACE SMA systems hit by mass exploitation campaign. 🛑
item.description:
Threat actors are systematically hijacking unpatched endpoint management appliances globally. 🛑
CVE-2025-32975 · Severity 10.0 · Active Exploitation in Quest KACE SMA.
Arctic Wolf has warned of a surge in malicious activity targeting unpatched @[Quest Software](urn:li:organization:2353) KACE Systems Management Appliances (SMA). Attackers are exploiting a critical authentication bypass to impersonate administrative users and gain full root control over the appliance.
Because KACE SMA is used for centralized software distribution and patching, root access here allows an attacker to push malware to every connected endpoint in the enterprise fleet. The campaign has specifically impacted the education sector in the last 24 hours.
The uncomfortable truth: Your patching tools are the most dangerous entry points for an attacker because they operate with the highest privileges and are often trusted implicitly by every device on your network.
→ Update Quest KACE SMA to the latest patched version immediately. → Strictly isolate the KACE SMA management interface from the public internet using a secure VPN or Zero Trust gateway. → Audit your appliance logs for the creation of unauthorized administrative accounts or anomalous software distribution tasks.
#Cybersecurity #EndpointSecurity #Quest #VulnerabilityManagement #SOC #CodeDefence

item.title: CISA orders federal agencies to patch DarkSword iOS and Craft CMS flaws. 📱
item.description:
Five critical vulnerabilities being used in active cyberespionage and code injection attacks have been added to the KEV catalog. 📱
KEV Update · DarkSword exploit kit targeted · Remediation Deadline: April 3, 2026.
The @[CISA](urn:li:organization:13010360) has added five security flaws to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. This includes a maximum-severity code injection in Craft CMS (CVE-2025-32432) and multiple @[Apple](urn:li:organization:162479) WebKit flaws (CVE-2025-31277) that are core components of the DarkSword iOS exploit kit.
These vulnerabilities are being used to compromise mobile devices and web platforms remotely. DarkSword allows for the silent exfiltration of passwords, Health data, and crypto wallets, while the CMS flaws enable unauthenticated command execution on web servers.
The uncomfortable truth: Your mobile browser and public-facing CMS are the primary entry points for modern state-sponsored intrusion sets.
→ Update all @[Apple](urn:li:organization:162479) devices to the latest iOS 26.3.1 or macOS 16.4.1 releases today to neutralize WebKit exploits. → Patch Craft CMS and Laravel Livewire instances to their latest versions immediately to prevent unauthenticated code injection. → Enable Lockdown Mode for high-value targets to specifically reduce the attack surface for complex memory corruption flaws.
#Cybersecurity #AppSec #MobileSecurity #Apple #CraftCMS #CodeDefence

item.title: Microsoft warns of IRS-themed phishing delivering RMM malware to 29,000 users. 🐚
item.description:
Attackers are exploiting tax-season urgency to deploy remote management tools across corporate networks. 🐚
Phishing Alert · 29,000 users targeted with malicious refund and filing reminders.
@[Microsoft](urn:li:organization:1035) has identified a massive phishing campaign masquerading as IRS refund notices and filing reminders. The campaign tricks users into opening malicious attachments that deploy legitimate Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) tools, providing the attacker with persistent, backdoor access to the endpoint.
By using legitimate RMM software, the attackers can bypass many traditional antivirus detections. Once established, they move laterally to siphon credentials and prepare for ransomware deployment.
The uncomfortable truth: Your users' natural concern for their financial and legal compliance is a weapon that attackers use to bypass your most expensive security controls.
→ Educate employees specifically on tax-themed lures and the danger of clicking "Refund" or "Correction" links in unsolicited emails. → Implement strict application control policies to block the execution of unauthorized RMM tools on non-IT workstations. → Audit your network for unusual outbound connections originating from common remote management binary names.
#Cybersecurity #Phishing #Microsoft #SocialEngineering #SOC #CodeDefence
